glossary.def: A group of uncrewed aircraft operating as a coordinated single operation under a common autonomy stack. In the input, the term is used throughout but is never numerically bounded; CAA-US-014 caps the airborne count but no story defines the minimum or the coordination protocol that qualifies a group as a 'swarm' vs. a 'formation'.
Beyond Visual Line of Sight. Operation where the remote pilot in command cannot see the aircraft directly. Used throughout but not defined in the stories.

The set of conditions under which the autonomous system is designed to operate safely (lat/long polygon, altitude band, wind, visibility, daylight). Named in CAA-US-005 but the schema of a 'machine-checkable' ODD is not prescribed.

Specific Operations Risk Assessment — the JARUS 2.0 methodology the CAA uses to determine Ground Risk Class, Air Risk Class and Specific Assurance and Integrity Level for Specific-category UAS operations.
Ground Risk Class — SORA Step 2 output expressing intrinsic ground risk before mitigation.
Air Risk Class — SORA Step 4 output expressing intrinsic airspace encounter risk.
Specific Assurance and Integrity Level — SORA Step 7 output mapping residual risk to required Operational Safety Objectives (OSO).
Operational Safety Objective — SORA-prescribed mitigation required at a given SAIL and robustness level.
Concept of Operations — document describing mission profile, ODD, roles and contingencies.
Temporary Danger Area — airspace construct applied via CAP 1616 / ANO to segregate a defined volume.
Command and Control Link — the datalink over which the RPIC commands the aircraft. Classed under RTCA DO-377 / STANAG 4660.
Detect and Avoid — the function that identifies and resolves a collision hazard with other traffic, per DO-365C / ASTM F3442.

Not defined in the corpus. Stories conflate 'autonomous', 'automated', 'AI-driven' and 'learned' without drawing the line between pre-coded behaviour and machine-learned behaviour. This is significant because assurance technique (AMLAS vs. conventional software assurance) hinges on the classification.

Behaviour that arises from interaction of swarm members and cannot be traced to an explicit rule in any single member. Used in CAA-US-013 and CAA-US-015 but the 'emergent vs. coded' distinction is not operationalised.

UK MOD policy position under which a human retains authority over any engagement decision and can intervene. Cited at CAA-US-019; however, the input does not define which classes of decision are 'in scope' for human authorisation vs. delegable to the autonomy.

Responsible AI Senior Officer — defined under JSP 936 Part 1. Cited in CAA-US-001, CAA-US-002, CAA-US-030 but the CAA-facing responsibilities of the RAISO (what they counter-sign, when) are not fully enumerated.
A decision point at the end of a trial phase. Cited in CAA-US-032 but the decision criteria are not specified.

Action that keeps the aircraft within declared volumes, e.g. geofence-triggered RTB, autoland or flight termination. Cited at CAA-US-008 and CAA-US-021. The input does not clarify whether 'containment' and 'termination' are equivalent or hierarchical.
An unintended departure from the authorised airspace. Used in CAA-US-008 and CAA-US-023 without formal definition; no numeric threshold distinguishes a 'fly-away' from a 'containment event'.
An input crafted to induce incorrect ML behaviour (e.g. jamming, spoofed ADS-B, adversarial EO patches). Cited in CAA-US-004 and CAA-US-015 but not defined operationally.
An input outside the statistical distribution of the training data. Cited in CAA-US-015; detection mechanism is required but no performance target.
Prohibition on changing an ML model during the trial window. Cited in CAA-US-017; 'cryptographic hash' is required but the hash-verification workflow is not defined.
Artefacts (SHAP, LIME, attention maps) that justify a decision. Cited in CAA-US-018. The input does not specify which decisions are 'safety-relevant' enough to require explainability artefacts.
Named in CAA-US-019 as 'published workload limits' but no published reference is cited. The CAA has no published RPAS-swarm workload limit.
Role named in CAA-US-002 — conventional AOC term, but meaning under a Specific Category OA is not defined by the input.
Role named in CAA-US-002 and CAA-US-029 — not a CAP 722 term; the input introduces it without definition or competency reference.
The software that implements the aircraft's decision-making. Used informally; distinction from 'flight control software' is unclear.
Operational Authorisation issued under UK Reg 2019/947 Art 12. Referenced but not defined for swarm activity specifically.
Used in CAA-US-019 ('an engagement or threat response') — a defence-specific term the civil regulator is unlikely to apply; overloads the civil reviewer's vocabulary.
The ground area under the aircraft's trajectory. CAA-US-028 uses 'ground-environment survey' but does not define the survey depth, vintage or recency requirement.

stories.title: Feature: Application Intake & Completeness Check
stories.story: As a CAA case officer, I want to receive a complete Operational Authorisation pack so that I can open a valid case.

stories.title: Feature: SORA 2.0 Assessment for Swarm
stories.story: As a CAA SORA assessor, I want swarm-aware SORA evidence so that I can set SAIL and OSO requirements defensibly.

stories.title: Feature: ConOps & ODD Review
stories.story: As a CAA safety-case reviewer, I want a clear ConOps and ODD so that I can judge nominal and worst-credible behaviour.

stories.title: Feature: Airspace Change & TDA Coordination
stories.story: As a CAA airspace regulator, I want a TDA and consultation package so that I can segregate the trial from other users.

stories.title: Feature: Geofence / Containment Enforcement
stories.story: As a CAA airspace regulator, I want automatic containment so that a fly-away cannot become uncontrolled BVLOS.

stories.title: Feature: C2 Link and Spectrum Assurance
stories.story: As a CAA spectrum specialist, I want authorised, monitored C2 and inter-vehicle links so that link loss is survivable and non-interfering.

stories.title: Feature: Detect-and-Avoid Performance
stories.story: As a CAA DAA assessor, I want DO-365 / F3442-aligned evidence for each aircraft and coordinated swarm de-confliction so that cooperative and non-cooperative encounters are resolved predictably.

stories.title: Feature: Swarm Decision-Making Architecture Review
stories.story: As a CAA autonomy reviewer, I want the decision-making architecture, airborne limits and emergent-behaviour evidence so that I can assure what the swarm will do collectively.

stories.title: Feature: ML Assurance Case
stories.story: As a CAA AI/ML assurance specialist, I want an AMLAS / SACE case, a frozen-model manifest and explainability evidence so that ML components are assurable and forensically explainable.

stories.title: Feature: Human Oversight and Crew Fitness
stories.story: As a CAA human factors inspector, I want workload, duty and take-over evidence so that meaningful human control is demonstrated throughout.

stories.title: Feature: Contingency and Flight Termination
stories.story: As a CAA flight-ops inspector, I want independent termination, a contingency matrix and ATSU coordination so that the trial can be made safe in any off-nominal state.

stories.title: Feature: Data, MOR & DPIA Obligations
stories.story: As a CAA flight safety investigator, I want complete recorded evidence, accepted MOR obligations and a DPIA so that any incident is investigable and privacy is protected.

stories.title: Feature: Insurance & Ground-Environment Survey
stories.story: As a CAA authorisation officer, I want insurance and a ground survey so that third-party risk is both transferable and factually grounded.

stories.title: Feature: Crew Training & Competency
stories.story: As a CAA licensing inspector, I want evidenced competency so that the trial is flown by qualified crew.

stories.title: Feature: CAA/MAA Interface Management
stories.story: As a CAA/MAA liaison, I want a signed apportionment so that regulatory ownership is gap-free.

stories.title: Feature: Phased Trial Build-Up
stories.story: As a CAA programme manager, I want phased gates so that evidence builds progressively and is reversible.

stories.title: Gap: MISSING: Public and Stakeholder Engagement
stories.story: No user story covers community consultation, press/media handling or prior-notification to landowners. Strongly implied by CAP 1616 and GDPR but absent.

stories.title: Gap: MISSING: Security Classification Handling
stories.story: No user story addresses how classified MOD evidence is presented to the CAA under CAP 722H / JSP 440 without compromise to either side.

stories.title: Feature: MISSING: Environmental Impact
stories.story: No user story covers noise, species disturbance at dusk/night, or battery-fire environmental risk on ground.

stories.title: Gap: MISSING: Post-Trial Assurance & Close-out
stories.story: No user story covers the post-trial lessons-learned, evidence preservation and authorisation close-out.

scenarios.given: A CAA case officer receives an OA pack for a BVLOS swarm trial.
scenarios.when: The officer runs the intake checklist.
scenarios.then: Every required artefact (SORA workbook, ConOps, RAISO letter, CAA/MAA routing memo, safety case, ML manifest, insurance certificate, DPIA) is present or a waiver is recorded, and the case is assigned a review team within 10 working days.

scenarios.given: The pack is missing the RAISO nomination and the ML deployment manifest.
scenarios.when: The officer runs the intake checklist.
scenarios.then: The case is not progressed, a deficiency letter is issued, and the applicant is given a defined window to resubmit.

scenarios.given: The applicant declares N airframes each with mass M and terminal velocity V.
scenarios.when: The SORA assessor calculates worst-credible simultaneous loss of control.
scenarios.then: The assessor uses the aggregated KE envelope rather than the single-aircraft KE, and the GRC is recomputed accordingly.

scenarios.given: The swarm uses identical hardware and identical autonomy stacks.
scenarios.when: The assessor evaluates independence of airframe reliability figures.
scenarios.then: Independence is rejected unless the applicant has enumerated common-cause failure modes (software defect, GNSS, RF, adversarial) and mitigated each.

scenarios.given: An airspace change is proposed with less than 90 days to the first sortie.
scenarios.when: The airspace regulator receives the application.
scenarios.then: The application is returned to the applicant because AIP SUP / NOTAM cycles cannot be met; a revised trial start date is required.

scenarios.given: One airborne member approaches the TDA boundary.
scenarios.then: Automatic containment executes before the boundary is crossed; the trigger-to-actuation budget is less than the time-to-boundary at max airspeed; NATS and the CAA duty officer are notified within the documented SLA.

scenarios.given: C2 link quality drops below the declared threshold.
scenarios.then: The RPIC is alerted with time, latency and recoverability data, and the documented contingency (hover, orbit, RTB, terminate) auto-initiates if a secondary threshold is crossed.

scenarios.given: Two or more members lose the inter-vehicle link.
scenarios.then: Each member falls back to standalone DO-365-compliant DAA behaviour, and the swarm degrades gracefully to independent operation.

scenarios.given: A GA aircraft without ADS-B enters the Operational Volume.
scenarios.when: At least one swarm member detects the intruder.
scenarios.then: The coordinated de-confliction protocol resolves a unique, predictable manoeuvre within the reaction-time budget, and no other member creates a cascading conflict.

scenarios.given: A swarm member encounters a weather or sensor condition outside its ODD or training distribution.
scenarios.then: The member executes a pre-declared safe response (hover, orbit, return, terminate) and the swarm coordinator propagates the state change to all members.

scenarios.given: A pre-flight deployment check runs against the ML manifest.
scenarios.when: The hash of a flown model does not match the manifest.
scenarios.then: Flight is prohibited until the discrepancy is resolved, and the occurrence is reported as an ML-specific anomaly.

scenarios.given: The RPIC issues a take-over command during active autonomy.
scenarios.then: The swarm transitions to the commanded state within the declared latency budget and the event is logged with a time-stamp.

scenarios.given: A C2 loss and a sensor failure occur on the same airframe within the same decision cycle.
scenarios.then: Priority ordering applies deterministically (termination > containment > RTB > orbit), and the outcome is logged.

scenarios.given: An occurrence that meets Reg 376/2014 criteria happens in flight.
scenarios.then: An MOR is filed via ECCAIRS within 72 hours.

scenarios.given: Emergent swarm behaviour is observed but no safety event results.
scenarios.then: A supplementary report is sent to the CAA even though it is not a Reg 376 occurrence.

scenarios.given: An incident straddles civil and military airspace.
scenarios.then: The joint CAA/MAA investigation path pre-agreed in the apportionment memo is activated; DSA and RAISO are notified simultaneously.

scenarios.given: A phase exit criterion is not met.
scenarios.then: The pre-declared regression path is activated — the next phase is not authorised and the applicant returns to a safer phase.

findings.title: F-D-01 — The core noun 'swarm' is never formally defined

sections.body: Every other story leans on this term. Without a definition, 'swarm limits' (CAA-US-014), 'swarm-level DAA' (CAA-US-012) and 'swarm-wide termination' (CAA-US-021) are each interpretable differently.
Used as if self-explanatory in every story, but with no lower/upper bounds on member count, no coordination-protocol threshold, and no distinction from 'formation' or 'coordinated flight'.

A group of two or more uncrewed aircraft operating under a single OA, sharing a common coordination protocol and a single RPIC, such that loss of coordination with the group is itself a safety-relevant event.

findings.title: F-P-02 — 'Defined latency budget' is used without a budget

sections.body: A 'budget' without a number is an uncheckable acceptance criterion.
Multiple stories (CAA-US-008, CAA-US-012, CAA-US-019, CAA-US-021) cite 'declared latency budget' / 'reaction-time budget' without a numeric value.
Follow DO-365C Well-Clear timing (approx. 35 s) for DAA, and DO-377 for C2 latency.
The latency budget is whatever the autonomy stack happens to achieve on the day.

findings.title: F-O-01 — The 'go/no-go' outcome has no enumerated decision set

sections.body: An outcome that is enumerable as a choice but is not enumerated cannot be implemented consistently across CAA reviewers.
CAA-US-032 declares that 'the CAA reviews the evidence and issues an explicit go/no-go' but does not enumerate the set of possible decisions (go, no-go, go-with-conditions, pause, terminate).

findings.title: F-O-04 — Outcome of a model update during the trial is under-specified

sections.body: Detecting the breach without a stated response risks ad-hoc handling and regulatory inconsistency.
CAA-US-017 says updates are 'not permitted without a fresh authorisation' — but does not say what happens if an update is detected mid-trial.

findings.title: F-S-01 — No scenario for partial-swarm return after partial fly-away

sections.body: CAA-US-008 covers the containment of a member and CAA-US-021 covers group termination, but the middle case (one down, rest continue) is unspecified.

Given the swarm has N members airborne and one experiences a containment event; When that member initiates containment; Then the remaining members' behaviour (continue mission, hold, group-return) must be specified.

findings.title: F-P-01 — 'Published workload limits' is not predictable

sections.body: Neither the CAA nor EASA publishes workload limits for multi-vehicle uncrewed operations; the acceptance criterion is therefore unprovable as written.
CAA-US-019: 'a single RPIC can monitor all airborne members within published workload limits'.
The applicant uses NASA-TLX or SART to demonstrate workload under a stated threshold, and proposes a cap based on that.
The limit is whatever the applicant's own CEO decides is acceptable.

findings.title: F-M-01 — No numeric thresholds for SAIL assignment

sections.body: SAIL is foundational to every downstream OSO level of evidence.
The stories invoke SORA SAIL but never specify the expected SAIL band for this trial (likely SAIL III or IV).
CAA-US-003 or a new constraint story
Applicant and reviewer negotiate SAIL from scratch, costing months.

findings.title: F-M-03 — No story for pre-flight airworthiness release

sections.body: The stories assume CAA is sufficient; for this trial the MAA is the airworthiness regulator.
For a military RPAS under RA 1600 and DEFSTAN 00-970 Part 9, a Release-to-Service / Authorised Service Modification is required before first flight. No story captures the airworthiness release chain.
Between CAA-US-001 and CAA-US-003
Without the release, the trial cannot legally launch even if all CAA evidence is complete.

findings.title: F-D-02 — 'Autonomous' / 'automated' / 'AI-driven' are used interchangeably

sections.body: Assurance method (AMLAS vs. conventional 00-055) hinges on this distinction.
CAA-US-013 refers to 'autonomy stack'; CAA-US-016 says 'AI/ML Assurance'; CAA-US-017 says 'ML model'. The collapse of autonomy-level vocabulary obscures which assurance regime applies.
Use JSP 936 definitions: 'ML component' = trained model; 'deterministic component' = coded rules; 'autonomy level' per ALFUS scale.

findings.title: F-D-03 — 'Swarm Commander' is coined without a competency reference

sections.body: The competency evidence at CAA-US-029 cannot be assessed without a definition of the role.
Introduced in CAA-US-002 and CAA-US-029 as a role distinct from RPIC and Accountable Manager but not defined anywhere in CAP 722.
A named role holding command authority over swarm-level decisions (formation change, role assignment, group contingency) while the RPIC retains individual-airframe authority.

findings.title: F-F-01 — Community consultation / public engagement is a missing feature

sections.body: CAP 1616 airspace change explicitly requires consultation. CAA-US-007 mentions 'affected airspace users' but not the public or landowners under the Operational Volume.
As a CAA airspace regulator, I want evidence that the public, landowners and affected airspace users have been consulted, so that CAP 1616 requirements are met and the trial has social licence.

findings.title: F-F-02 — Post-trial close-out is a missing feature

sections.body: Every story covers pre- or in-flight activity. No story covers ending the trial, archiving evidence, or feeding lessons back into CAP 722.

As a CAA programme manager, I want a defined close-out artefact (lessons-learned, evidence preservation, authorisation withdrawal), so that the trial authorisation does not silently over-run its validity.

findings.title: F-F-04 — Security-classification handling is absent

sections.body: The trial is MOD-sponsored (Alvina context). CAP 722H is cited but no story covers how OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE / SECRET evidence is provided to the CAA.
As a CAA safety-case reviewer, I want a defined pathway to receive, store and review classified MOD evidence, so that I can assure the trial without either side compromising its security regime.

findings.title: F-P-03 — 'Demonstrated link margin' has no numeric threshold

sections.body: Without a number the acceptance criterion is not falsifiable.
CAA-US-009: 'a minimum link margin has been demonstrated at maximum range'.
Require ≥ 6 dB fade margin at maximum range with 95 % availability over the sortie window, per typical CAP 722 practice.
Any positive link budget is acceptable.

findings.title: F-A-01 — Vague quantifier 'extensive'

sections.body: 'Extensive' is the textbook DeFOSPAM weasel word — it shifts the burden of proof to the regulator.
CAA-US-013: 'extensive Monte Carlo or agent-based simulation'
Sufficient to demonstrate convergence at 95 % CI over the ODD.
A weekend run on a laptop because it 'felt extensive'.

findings.title: F-A-02 — 'Where practicable' is a get-out clause

sections.body: 'Where practicable' without a definition of impracticability permits circular reasoning.
CAA-US-021: 'functionally and physically independent of the autonomy software and uses a separate RF path where practicable.'
If the applicant can show a separate RF path is technically infeasible, a shared path is acceptable.
The applicant self-declares 'not practicable' and the CAA cannot challenge it.

findings.title: F-D-04 — 'Containment' and 'termination' are used without a hierarchy

sections.body: A developer cannot implement the stories without knowing whether containment succeeds include termination or stop short of it.
CAA-US-008 uses 'containment' for geofence-triggered actions; CAA-US-021 uses 'termination' as an independent function; CAA-US-022 references both without ordering.

Containment: keep the aircraft within declared volumes (may include RTB, orbit, autoland). Termination: intentional cessation of flight (forced landing or destructive). Termination is a subset of containment but is always available independently.

findings.title: F-F-03 — Night operations and dusk/dawn are implicit

sections.body: CAA-US-005 mentions 'daylight' in the ODD example but no story constrains dusk, dawn or night explicitly — yet EO-sensor-based DAA (CAA-US-011) is strongly light-dependent.
As a CAA flight-ops inspector, I want the authorisation to state permitted light levels, so that the DAA / EO sensor performance envelope is matched to the actual flight window.

findings.title: F-O-02 — Null outcome for inadvertent personal-data capture is not stated

sections.body: Without a declared in-flight response, post-flight redaction is the only control — too late if storage itself is the risk.
CAA-US-026 asks for a DPIA and redaction procedure but does not state what the system does at the moment of inadvertent capture (e.g. whether the sensor continues recording or auto-masks).

findings.title: F-S-02 — No scenario for concurrent DAA and C2-loss events

sections.body: CAA-US-022 lists the matrix generically but does not test this combination explicitly.

Given a DAA conflict has been detected and a C2 link loss occurs within the resolution time-budget; When the swarm executes a cooperative avoidance manoeuvre; Then the avoidance manoeuvre must complete autonomously without RPIC concurrence.

findings.title: F-S-03 — No scenario for operations in reduced visibility or icing

sections.body: CAA-US-005 mentions wind and visibility as ODD parameters but does not specify the response to degraded conditions.
Given visibility or icing conditions exceed the declared ODD; When any member encounters these conditions; Then a coordinated response moves the swarm out of the deteriorating volume or lands it.

findings.title: F-P-04 — 'Confidence intervals' without a level are unpredictable

sections.body: The acceptance criterion rewards or penalises applicants arbitrarily.
CAA-US-016 requires ML metrics 'expressed as confidence intervals' but no level (90 %, 95 %, 99 %) is stated.
Require 95 % CI or matched to SAIL level.
Any non-trivial interval is accepted.

findings.title: F-M-02 — No CRUD coverage for authorisation conditions

sections.body: The OA is a living instrument; the stories treat it as static.
Stories describe issue of conditions (create) and review at phase gates (read), but there is no 'update a condition mid-trial' or 'withdraw an authorisation' story.
Adjacent to CAA-US-032
Neither the CAA nor the applicant knows how conditions change when new evidence emerges.

findings.title: F-M-04 — No non-functional performance envelope declared

sections.body: DO-178C, DEFSTAN 00-055 and AMLAS all require timing assurance for safety-relevant software.
Performance NFRs for the autonomy stack (decision cycle period, worst-case execution time, CPU/GPU utilisation) are absent.
CAA-US-013 or a new story
A timing-induced failure (deadline miss) is indistinguishable from an algorithmic failure in post-incident analysis.

findings.title: F-M-05 — No cyber-resilience test story

sections.body: The autonomy stack, GCS and C2 link are all soft attack surfaces.
CAP 722H is cited in passing but no story asks for penetration testing, a cyber-incident response plan, or SBOM.
Adjacent to CAA-US-004 / CAA-US-009
A cyber event during the trial has no pre-declared containment.

findings.title: F-M-07 — No authority to inspect, suspend or revoke between phases

sections.body: CAP 722 and ANO give the CAA powers of inspection; the stories do not exercise them.
The CAA's right to inspect the applicant's facility, suspend flights pending inspection, or revoke between phases is absent.
CAA-US-032 or a new powers story
Phase gating without inspection is a rubber-stamp exercise.

findings.title: F-O-03 — No outcome stated when the applicant refuses an authorisation condition

sections.body: The CAA's escalation path (refuse OA, offer a reduced scope OA, refer to MAA) is itself an outcome class.
The stories describe conditions the CAA will impose (CAA-US-008 SLA, CAA-US-014 numeric caps, CAA-US-017 model freeze) but never state what happens if the applicant refuses.

findings.title: F-S-04 — No data-driven scenario table for airborne-count limits

sections.body: CAA-US-014 specifies the limit as a numeric condition but does not table the enforcement behaviour.
Scenario Outline: airborne count limits — Given N airborne members, When a Nth+1 launch is attempted, Then the launch is inhibited at the GCS.

findings.title: F-P-05 — 'Monte Carlo statistics' without sample-size floor

sections.body: Without a floor the regulator cannot compare applicants.
CAA-US-013 asks for 'behavioural envelope statistics' from Monte Carlo or agent-based simulation.
Require sample size sufficient to estimate failure rate at SAIL-appropriate confidence (e.g. 3 × 10⁻⁴ for SAIL IV).
A single simulation run is treated as evidence.

findings.title: F-M-06 — No story for human-in-the-loop testing of the GCS

sections.body: Measured workload under realistic failure scenarios is the HF community's expected evidence.
CAA-US-019 references workload limits but does not require live human-in-the-loop testing of the GCS with the operational crew under off-nominal scenarios.
Adjacent to CAA-US-019/020
Workload analysis is modelled but not measured.

findings.title: F-D-05 — The defence term 'engagement' is used in a civil-regulator context

sections.body: Mixing civil and defence vocabulary invites misreading and undermines the CAA/MAA apportionment memo in CAA-US-030.
CAA-US-019 acceptance criterion references 'an engagement or threat response' — this is defence vocabulary that does not map onto CAA decision-making.
Replace with 'a safety-critical or use-of-force decision' so the CAA reviewer does not have to interpret kinetic terminology.

findings.title: F-A-03 — 'Pre-briefed' vs. 'pre-declared' used inconsistently

sections.body: Auditability hinges on this distinction — oral briefings are not evidence.
CAA-US-023 uses 'pre-briefed'; CAA-US-008, CAA-US-015 and CAA-US-022 use 'pre-declared'. CAA-US-032 uses 'pre-declared'.
Pre-briefed means an oral briefing occurred; pre-declared means a written declaration exists.
They are the same thing and the drafter switched words for variety.

findings.title: F-F-05 — Environmental and noise assessment is missing

sections.body: CAA-US-028 touches ground environment but not wider environmental impact.

As a CAA environmental policy team member, I want an environmental assessment (noise, species disturbance, battery-fire ground risk), so that the trial does not breach DEFRA / Natural England expectations.

findings.title: F-A-04 — Weasel word 'reasonable' hidden in story 029

sections.body: 'Recent' without a period is the most common currency-definition trap in UK aviation.
CAA-US-029: 'recent simulator and live-flying currency records exist'.
Recent = within the last 90 days per CAP 722 analogues.
Recent = any time this calendar year.

findings.title: F-A-05 — 'Significant multipath' contains undefined adjective

sections.body: The phrase passes any review that the applicant writes.
CAA-US-009: 'fade and multipath analysis' — the absence of a threshold turns the analysis into a narrative, not a pass/fail.
An FDTD-based multipath simulation against the terrain model, at 1 m resolution.
A back-of-envelope calculation of expected reflection loss.

findings.title: F-A-06 — 'Declared' vs. 'documented' used as synonyms but differ in legal weight

sections.body: Audit evidence turns on this distinction.
Multiple stories use both words. In regulator practice, a 'declaration' is a regulator-facing legal instrument; 'documented' can be internal-only.
Declared items must be counter-signed; documented items need only exist in the applicant's files.
They are treated as interchangeable by the reviewer.

findings.title: F-M-08 — No explicit evidence-preservation period post-trial

sections.body: AMLAS and CAP 722 both expect long-term evidence preservation.
CAA-US-024 sets retention for flight data, but wider evidence (assurance case, training records, ML manifests) has no retention period.
CAA-US-024 or close-out story
Post-trial re-investigation (e.g. if a failure manifests later) cannot be supported.
